key_departures.impact: Recorded 14.5 sacks over two seasons with Denver as a versatile 3-4 DE who could rush from inside or outside. Was the crown jewel of the Broncos' pass-rush rotation and one of the top DL free agents in the market. Denver never seriously pursued re-signing him given his expected price tag. His departure leaves a significant void at the 5-technique/IDL spot.

Six-year Broncos veteran who was a primary starter in 2024 (74 tackles, 3 TFLs in 15 starts) and stepped back into a starting role during the 2025 playoff run after Brandon Jones was lost to injury. His departure leaves Denver thinner at safety depth behind Talanoa Hufanga and Devon Key (ERFA tender).

Released by Denver after a disastrous one-year tenure. Signed a 3-year, $31.5M deal in 2025 but played only 8 games due to quad injuries and a one-game suspension. Designated as a post-June 1 cut, saving the Broncos $8.19M in 2026 cap space with $2.17M dead money. His release was effectively a cap cleanup — he was not a functional starter in 2025 — so his departure does not materially escalate the LB need given Singleton and Strnad were already re-signed.

Non-tendered as an RFA; the $3.52M tender was deemed too high for a backup. He was inactive for 9 of 10 games to start 2025 and only saw action after Dobbins went down. The Broncos still have interest in re-signing him at a lower price. His departure thins out the backfield beyond Dobbins and RJ Harvey but does not create a new critical need — more of a depth loss.

signings.contract_notes: Base value $16M; max value $20M with incentives (e.g., $2M for 1,200 scrimmage yards). $8M fully guaranteed in Year 1; Year 2 ($8M) is non-guaranteed, giving Denver an out. Reported by NFL Network/ESPN.
signings.analysis: Dobbins led the Broncos in carries (153), rushing yards (772), and explosive runs (21 of 10+ yards) before a Lisfranc injury ended his season in Week 10. He was on pace for 1,375+ scrimmage yards when healthy. At $10M AAV (base), he's in the mid-tier starter range for RB, but chronic injury history (has not played more than 13 games in a season since his 2020 rookie year) prevents this from being a 'fully addressed' designation. The 1-year effective guarantee means Denver has an exit if he breaks down again. They still need a complementary back alongside Dobbins and RJ Harvey — likely addressed via the draft.

signings.contract_notes: 2-year deal worth $15.5M with $11M guaranteed, including $3M signing bonus. Reported by Ian Rapoport/ESPN/Denver Gazette.
signings.analysis: Singleton had arguably his best professional season in 2025 with 135 tackles (team-leading) just weeks after cancer surgery. His knowledge of DC Vance Joseph's system is elite — the coaching staff likens it to having the DC in the huddle. However, he is 32 years old on a 2-year deal, which signals a bridge commitment rather than a long-term solution. Combined with Strnad's re-signing, the immediate LB need is addressed, but the Broncos will almost certainly draft a LB in the early rounds to ensure future continuity. The 2-year structure with $11M guaranteed means they could exit after 2026 if Singleton declines.

signings.contract_notes: 3-year deal worth up to $19.5M max (reported as $18M base) with $10M fully guaranteed. Reported by ESPN/Denver Gazette/Denver Post.
signings.analysis: Strnad emerged as a legitimate starter in 2025, starting 8 games in 2024 when Singleton was injured and building on that in 2025. The 3-year commitment at $6.5M AAV is team-friendly money for a legitimate starter and represents a real investment. Combined with the Singleton re-signing, Denver's ILB room is in better shape than it appeared entering free agency. Still, at 30 years old for Strnad and 32 for Singleton, drafting a long-term answer at LB is still advisable. The room is solidified short-term but not long-term.

signings.contract_notes: 3-year deal worth $17M (max $18.5M with incentives), $9.5M guaranteed. Source: Spotrac, Denver Post, Mike Klis/9News.
signings.analysis: Trautman is a trusted run-blocker and pass-protector in Sean Payton's system, having played 56 consecutive games for Denver (regular season + playoffs) without missing one. He caught 20 passes for 195 yards in 2025. However, at ~$5.7M AAV, his market tier is at the depth/low-starter range for TE. He is a reliable Y tight end who does the dirty work, not a receiving-first weapon. The Broncos still need a receiving TE upgrade — reports indicate the team was expected to remain active in the TE market. This re-signing fills a system role but does not upgrade the offensive production at the position.

signings.contract_notes: 1-year deal at the ERFA tender value of $1.075M. Reported by Denver Broncos official site and Denver Post.
signings.analysis: Adkins had drop issues in 2025 (3 drops on 9 catchable targets) but provides a versatile depth option at TE. One-year minimum-tier deal — purely a depth/camp roster body. Does not address the team's need for a receiving TE upgrade. No needle movement on TE need.

signings.contract_notes: 2-year deal, base value $9.5M (max $11M with incentives), $5.6M guaranteed. Source: Denver Post (Parker Gabriel), Washington Post, ProFootballRumors.
signings.analysis: Palczewski started 10 games at LG in 2025 filling in for injured Ben Powers and has shown versatility (LG, RG, RT capable). The 2-year deal at $4.75M AAV is team-friendly and keeps a proven swing starter locked up at below-market cost. This is a depth/insurance signing — Ben Powers ($18.15M cap hit) remains the expected starting LG in 2026. Palczewski provides excellent depth insurance but does not move the needle on any pressing IOL need.

signings.contract_notes: 1-year, $2M deal. Reported by Ian Rapoport/Tom Pelissero (NFL Network) and Denver Post.
signings.analysis: Ehlinger is Bo Nix's backup with Jarrett Stidham potentially a trade candidate per reports. One-year deal cements the backup QB depth for 2026 at minimal cap cost. No impact on team needs — QB is not a pressing need with Nix locked in as the starter.

signings.contract_notes: 1-year deal. Terms not publicly reported. Source: NFL.com free agency tracker.
signings.analysis: Veteran long snapper re-signed to maintain roster continuity. No impact on team needs.

signings.contract_notes: ERFA tender at $1.075M. Signed the tender. Source: Denver Gazette.
signings.analysis: Reserve RB retained via ERFA tender. Camp/PS body and emergency depth behind Dobbins and Harvey. No meaningful needle movement on RB need.

signings.contract_notes: ERFA tender at $1.075M. Source: Denver Gazette/Denver Post.
signings.analysis: Primarily a special teams contributor who has earned expanding defensive roles. Retained at ERFA minimum. With Locke gone, Key provides the only depth behind Talanoa Hufanga and Brandon Jones at safety. Does not solve the S depth hole created by Locke's departure — that need remains.

signings.contract_notes: ERFA tender at $1.075M. Source: Denver Gazette.
signings.analysis: Developmental OLB depth retained at minimum cost. No impact on needs.

signings.contract_notes: ERFA tender, reportedly tendered per Denver Gazette (some uncertainty noted in sourcing). $1.075M.
signings.analysis: Interior DL depth retained at minimum. Provides developmental depth behind Zach Allen and Malcolm Roach. Does not address the hole left by Franklin-Myers' departure at all.

signings.contract_notes: 2nd-round RFA tender, worth $5.81M for 2026. Source: Denver Post, SI/On3. Team retains right to match any offer sheet.
signings.analysis: McMillian is Denver's starting nickel corner, tendered at the 2nd-round level ($5.81M) to prevent any team from poaching him without compensation. He had a crucial interception in the divisional round vs. Buffalo. CB was not a pressing need with Patrick Surtain II locked in at CB1, and McMillian retained at nickel. This tender maintains the status quo at a position of strength. Not a needle-mover for team needs.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: John Franklin-Myers (14.5 sacks in 2 seasons, elite pass-rush versatility) signed a 3yr/$63M deal with Tennessee. Denver made no replacement signing. The remaining IDL room (Zach Allen, Malcolm Roach, Jordan Jackson) is solid but loses its top pass-rush interior weapon. IDL escalates from a 'monitor in the draft' need to a genuine draft priority — likely a Day 2 selection to replace JFM's production and versatility. Was already partially a need; JFM's departure makes it more urgent.

P.J. Locke (1-year, $5M Cowboys deal) was a 6-year Denver veteran who served as the primary safety in the 2025 playoffs after Brandon Jones' injury. His departure creates a depth hole behind Talanoa Hufanga and Devon Key (ERFA minimum). With Brandon Jones' health uncertain and Key unproven as a starter, S now represents a genuine need for at least a mid-tier veteran or Day 2 draft pick. Escalated from depth consideration to an actual need.

The double re-signing of Alex Singleton (2yr/$15.5M, $11M gtd) and Justin Strnad (3yr/$19.5M max, $10M gtd), combined with the release of the failed Dre Greenlaw experiment, leaves the ILB room better than it appeared entering FA. Starting tandem is set for at least 2026. However, Singleton is 32 on a 2-year deal and Strnad turns 30 in August — the Broncos will still target a LB early in the draft (Rounds 1-3) for long-term depth, but the immediate need is reduced from tier 2 to tier 3.

J.K. Dobbins re-signed (2yr/$20M max, $8M gtd). When healthy, he was a top-5 RB (5.0 YPC, 21 explosive runs in 10 games). He gives the Broncos a legitimate No. 1 back alongside RJ Harvey. However, Dobbins has never played 14+ games in a season, and McLaughlin was non-tendered, leaving the depth thin. The need drops from a critical tier-2 to a tier-3 'add depth/youth via the draft' need. Denver will very likely draft a RB in Rounds 2-4 as Payton identified RB as a 'must' at the Combine.

Trautman's 3yr/$17M re-signing and Adkins' 1-year minimum deal do not move the needle on the TE receiving need. Trautman is a trusted blocker and short-yardage target but not a receiving weapon. Evan Engram (cap hit ~$14M, with drop issues) may or may not return. Denver was still expected to pursue TE options in free agency or the draft. The TE need is unchanged — they need a receiver at the position, and Trautman does not provide that.

No WR additions made. Denver monitored Romeo Doubs (signed with New England at ~$70M base) and Kendrick Bourne but made no moves. Courtland Sutton is the clear WR1, but the team lacks a legitimate WR2. The Broncos passed on overpaying in FA and appear to be targeting WR in the draft. Need remains unchanged.

Palczewski's 2yr/$9.5M re-signing provides swing-starter depth, and Ben Powers ($18.15M cap hit) returns as the expected starting LG. Luke Wattenberg is locked in at center. IOL is not a pressing need — this remains a depth/development consideration unchanged by FA.

key_additions.headline: Dobbins returns as the RB1 after a top-5 pace in 2025 (5.0 YPC) ended by a Lisfranc injury; club banking on health in 2026
Team captain and defensive cornerstone re-signs after 135-tackle season; brings unmatched system knowledge to Vance Joseph's defense
First multi-year deal for Strnad locks in the ILB starting tandem alongside Singleton after his breakout 2025 campaign
Reliable Payton-system TE re-signs on a 3-year deal; has not missed a single game in 3 Broncos seasons (56-of-56 including playoffs)
Versatile O-line Swiss Army knife locked up at team-friendly value after starting 10 games at LG when Ben Powers was injured
Breakout nickel corner tendered at 2nd-round level to protect key playoff performer from being poached in free agency

key_losses.headline: Denver's most disruptive interior pass rusher (14.5 sacks over 2 seasons) departs for a massive payday with Tennessee; top IDL need now unaddressed
Six-year Broncos veteran and playoff starter departs for Dallas, creating a safety depth void behind Talanoa Hufanga
One-year bust released after playing just 8 games on a 3yr/$31.5M deal; Broncos eat $2.17M dead cap and save $8.19M post-June 1
Fan-favorite reserve RB non-tendered to avoid $3.52M RFA price tag; Broncos retain interest in re-signing at lower cost

notes: Denver's 2026 free agency strategy was almost entirely 'run it back' — re-signing 7+ of their own free agents while making zero external additions through the first two days of the legal tampering period and Day 1 of the new league year (March 11). The team appears to be saving cap space for the draft and potential mid-to-late free agency additions. Bo Nix extension planning (which will be a monster deal) likely informs this conservative approach. Cap space post-FA estimated at ~$20M based on Denver Post and Mile High Report calculations after all reported signings are factored in; Greenlaw's $8.19M savings does not materialize until post-June 1.
